speaker: interviewer
text: It's February the 15th, 1996. Interviewer Shirley Eisenberg, of the survivor Paul Kornmehl. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia, and it's an English language interview. I'm Shirley Eisenberg. I'm conducting an interview with Paul Kornmehl. It's the 15th of February, 1996, the language is English, and the interview is being conducted in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Paul Kornmehl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-R-N-M-E-H-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Scheveningen, which is the coast of the Hague. In

speaker: interviewer
text: Which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now, Paul?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm now 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as far as I know. My-- the name I've been booked in, in Holland, by my father, was not Paul, but was Pinkas, P-I-N-K-A-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, my parents came to Holland in 1916 with two daughters. The one was Bertha, the other one Ana. They were both born in Tarnow in Poland. My mother came from Kolomea, also Poland. I was-- my brother was born in Holland, by the name Isaac, in 1917, and I was born in 1920 in a street called-- in a street called [NON-ENGLISH], which was opposite a very religious synagogue. My parents were very, very religious. If my father didn't travel-- I'm not talking of the 1920s, but even later on-- he went to shul in the morning and in the evening. We were strictly kosher, and Holland, like everywhere else in the 1920s, was very, very difficult to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father, mother, and family left for a place in Germany called Chemnitz, where he started a hosiery factory with his partner, [? Hagler. ?] And the hosiery company was called Hako, H-A-K-O. What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1922. And we stayed, and I went to school when I was four or five years of age in a German school. We left Germany in 1928, and we went to Belgium. And we stayed in Antwerp-- well, we lived in Antwerp. I believe and I understand, and I saw it with my own eyes that my father had a very, very difficult time. What he exactly did, I think, he was buying and selling hosiery. We were always in hosiery, the whole family. I went to a very religious school where I had-- I was taught Hebrew, Flemish, and French. We stayed in Belgium from 1928 until 1932, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then we went back to Holland, where my father found somebody who financed him and he went back into the hosiery game. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Talking about my father and my mother, that's what you want me to talk about, right? My father was a very hardworking man. He sometimes left in the morning at 4:00, and he had an arrangement as a conductor in the train, that he got a compartment for himself so that-- that he can put on the tefillin. But when he was in-- we again lived in Scheveningen, not in the Hague, which is the same, really-- when he was not going on a trip, you know, traveling around selling hosiery and so on, then he went to the shul in the morning and in the shul in the evening, in the synagogue. Now, what else do you want to ask me? How did you spend your time in those early years outside school hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend your time after school with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: After school, we went to play soccer. I think I had some tennis lessons. I didn't pay for that. And my father pushed me to study Hebrew. Right? It was-- I was one of the few in the family that was not very religious, right, or the only one, really. What's your next question?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my bar mitzvah. I studied this for a long, long time, and at the shul, the synagogue, which was in the house in [INAUDIBLE], and we were on the first floor, which were even more religious one than the one downstairs. Downstairs there were some which didn't have a beard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my bar mitzvah was not really eventful, right? It didn't do anything to me. But it was the year, and one had to do it, right? And then in the afternoon, I had a [? Shia, ?] which stands for that I had to learn, again, Hebrew, which I had also on a Sunday and which I had probably in between Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday after school, and that was rather difficult. You didn't ask me which school I went to. Which school did you go to at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, for some odd reason, wanted me to go to German school. And that school was called [GERMAN] Why German, I don't know. That was 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your experiences in that school.

speaker: interviewee
text: My experience in that school was that they looked towards me as being an outsider because, by 1933, the teacher, as well as the headmaster, they were all Nazis. In 1934, I wasn't-- in 1934, they were singing in the gymnastic hall some of the German songs, and they were holding their hands up. I told it to my mother. And my mother was closer to me than my father, because my father was always trying to make ends meet. And we had a reasonably good income. Now I'm getting back to school first, right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes, yes, they were antisemitic. And this repeated itself '35, '36, and '37. I think I left school in 1937. Not I think, I know I left it because I passed into high school. But up till the last day, whenever there was a-- a meeting in the gymnastic hall, which was also used as a meeting place, they were all holding up their arms, you know. And the one song which I didn't hear was the I didn't hear that one, but all the other ones, they were holding up their arms. And I was happy that I left school in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends amongst the other students?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I did not have any friends, because I think that there might have been only one or two Jews among so many hundreds of others. And I still don't understand why my mother insisted I should go to that school. But at the same time, I learned very well German, and I spoke already French because of Antwerp. And Dutch was my mother's language, that wasn't a problem, so it helped me along the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience antisemitism from the other students?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I did not experience that. That was not-- I can't say that. Right, I met some of them after the war, or one in particular, and it was never-- it never was, you know, never mentioned to me that I was a Jew and they were Christians, or they were Nazis, or anything else. But according to me, they were all pro-Hitler. There was no doubt about it. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any youth groups outside school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was involved in a Zionist organization. We had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how would I say-- camping on a Sunday, because on Shabbat I was not supposed to travel, in places like [GERMAN] and we left early in the morning by train, and we were all singing our Hebrew songs there. The Zionist organization was very strong in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of any of your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the one which I like to mention, and that was in 1942, we went to Amsterdam. And I met there a number of friends, and one of them was Anne Frank. And she spoke Dutch with a very heavy German accent. This I remember very, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: I forgot about it until after the war. They were talking about her book, and the man who was with me, which I remember going to Amsterdam, was Herbert Cohen, which unfortunately, died a year ago. And he was rather hero in the resistance, in the Dutch resistance in Holland. Would you like me to ask me why? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you why. Herbert Cohen was a very, very nice man. He came originally from Germany. He spoke German without an accent. His father was Dutch. His mother was German. And the Governor of Holland was Seyss-Inquart, who came from Austria. And he had a doctor, and that doctor had free entrance towards the [? plane, ?] towards the department where Seyss-Inquart had his residence, or his workplace. The doctor of Seyss-Inquart looked exactly like Herbert Cohen. And Herbert Cohen was asked by the resistance in Holland if he wouldn't mind going in there and taking some papers out. And he did that a few times. He was trained to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I personally think that he didn't realize in what danger he was. At the same time, I've got a lot of respect for him. He was extremely generous after the war. He built up an-- a huge business in computers and whatever goes with it, and he gave large amount of money, in millions and millions of dollars, towards Israel. Next question? Going back to your family, beyond your parents and your sisters and your brother, who were other significant members of the family in your youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a brother and I two sisters. My sister married with somebody from Chemnitz-- that was Bertha, the oldest one. And they had two children, which was Zeke and Manny. The other sister married somebody from Antwerp, which was Isaac Feigenbaum, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- who had a daughter in 1938 by the name of Paulette. And my brother married during the war time, but we are coming to that, are we?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember my grandparents. I think they were dead by the time-- when I was around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any aunts or uncles who were significant in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was one uncle who came from Kolomea, and his name was Uncle Schmiel, because whoever came from Poland was poor. Scheveningen was a very Jewish place because we probably only had a few thousand living there. I think 2,000, but I'm not sure about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we had a lot of people coming from other countries to collect money for themself, or for yeshivas, which were probably also for them because in Poland, people were very poor. They also came from other places like Czechoslovakia and Bulgaria and Romania to Scheveningen, and Scheveningen by itself was poor, right? We are talking about the 1930s, right, when there was a depression in the world. So there wasn't much there, really, but they went and, you know, people collected $0.10, $0.25, which for them was a meal, right? And so often they came and they said, we have got 12 children, 14 children at home. So with that little bit of money, which we had, we still gave some away. And I'm coming back to what I said before. My father had a good wage, which he collected every month on the first of the month. But we had suppliers of meat and-- not meat, really, chickens. We had three suppliers of chickens, and they needed money at the beginning of the month. So by the fifth, the sixth, and the seventh, we had to eat chicken. We had to eat eggs because they all wanted to pay us back. Whatever my father earned, because in Holland you don't get your wages every week like in Australia, but once a month. It's always in five, six, or seven days, our money was gone. Right? Also, the men who did our washing, right, they also needed money. So we had quite a number of people, but I think that was the whole economy in Scheveningen, and probably Holland, that you took it from-- one-- in one hand, you gave it out in the other hand, right? That's how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all together, we were what you call the [NON-ENGLISH]. We were not poor. We were not rich. We didn't have a car. We had bicycles. I went to school with a bicycle, right, from Scheveningen to the Hague, which everybody did in Holland. It was nothing unusual.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I can't say that-- you asked me that question, there's anything else towards this. What I forgot to tell you, Shirley, is that I left school in 1937 and I joined my father, working with him in the hosiery business. The hosiery business was we imported from Germany, we sold it in Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939-- and my father had a partner by the name of [INAUDIBLE], which also from Poland, and we couldn't take it that the man really didn't give us the right wage. So one day, as he sacked everybody every four or six weeks for the sake that we shouldn't come for an increase, we left him in 1938 or '39. I think in 1939. We left him from when we had our usual notice, which went on every few weeks. We left him, and my brother-in-law, who was still in Chemnitz-- no, it must have been 1938-- who was still in Chemnitz supplied us with hosiery, and we were selling it in Holland far below the price of my father. And so we were in competition with my father, who joined us in 1940. Right, just before the war. And Mr. [? Wong ?] was left with a business to himself, which was difficult for him, but he was a very, very wealthy man, so it wouldn't have made any difference to the man. By nature, he was very stingy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living in your family home at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was living in my families' home until the day that I left Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember your family home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was [NON-ENGLISH] 141, Scheveningen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a three bedroom apartment. Don't forget that my two sisters were married. And we were one of the few in Holland, or in Scheveningen, which had a bathroom, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] cold and hot water. We even had a maid. So, we were not what you would call poor, but there were people in Scheveningen which were better off, but most of them were worse off. Right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did the cooking at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. I mean, this was the Jewish family tradition that-- there was not a wife that goes out for a job or anything like that. My mother was in the kitchen most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite meal was always boiled chicken. Boiled chicken, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], right? Chopped liver, chopped herring, right? Gefilte fish, right? You mentioned that. That's what we had at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't forget my mother and my father came from Poland, right? So we had a traditional kosher meal from the olden days from Poland, or wherever. Right? So what's the next question, Shirley? In your late teens, towards 1939, what did you know of what was happening in the rest of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- what was happening where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the rest of Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the rest of Europe, we knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: The newspapers, the radio. We were not aware-- we were aware that the Jews were not treated well, but our feeling was, in Holland, that Holland has been neutral in the first World War. Whatever is going to happen now, Holland is going to be neutral. And I think the people in Belgium-- and I went up and down to Antwerp every few months-- were of the same opinion. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were convinced that we will stay out of the war. And we had the same feeling as what the Dutch, what the German Jews had towards the Polish Jews that they thought, the Germans, nothing will happen to us. It's the Polish Jews which were expelled from Germany in the Nazi time. I think the Dutch Jews had that same feeling. It was that they were not even against the German Jews, against the Polish Jews, which came to us, or wanted to escape or were waiting for a visa to go to America, or to South America, or to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of the Jews which came over illegally were put in a camp in Holland, but it wasn't what you would call a concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a camp more for transit. If they were caught close to the border, they were sent back to Germany, and that wasn't really very nice. And we felt very angry about it, but what could we do? We still, even if I was born in Holland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I still felt not-- at that particular time, later it was different-- that I was still a foreigner. Right? They didn't treat the foreign Jews, or who came over illegal, the way they should have been treated. They should have been treated as being [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, refuges, people which haven't got a home. And Holland could afford to look after them, right? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were very wealthy. Holland was very wealthy. We didn't have a deficit. We had a colony. You know, the Indonesia was our colony. At that time, they called it Nederlands West India. We were wealthy. So, but somehow along the line, there wasn't even that standing up of the Dutch Jews to help those people. It wasn't there. Thank you, Paul. We're going to change the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. OK.
